11. Gaming the algorithm is risky

Despite doing some stuff for seemingly random and arbitrary reasons, the algorithm is capable. It’s the same algorithm that draws millions of people into an endless rabbit hole of recommended videos. It’s the same algorithm that can act as a ‘big brother‘ that will censor certain words and sentiments. Sure, it’s often used in the wrong way, but you can’t say that it’s stupid.

But I didn’t know that. I re-released videos. I gamed the keywords. I released the same videos on different channels. Sure, it used to work in the olden days of the internet. Hell, it still even works today, albeit in the short term. But trust me, the algorithm will pick it up and penalize your channel without even noticing you. The good rule is: that if you do something that will confuse, disappoint, or upset your the algorithm will most likely see it and punish your channel.

Play it safe, don’t be greedy, and be honest with your audience.

12. Treat the YouTube algorithm as a business

Recommendations don’t come without strings attached. When YouTube recommends your videos, it expects you to make users stay as long as they can. If you fail to do so, they will penalize you and recommend someone else instead. Just like in business, you shouldn’t waste someone’s time when you don’t bring anything worthwhile in return. In this simile, you shouldn’t waste YouTube’s recommendations by releasing videos that your isn’t interested in. You would be better off uploading and playing it safe by releasing stuff you know most likely to work.

Note: this only applies when your audience is recommendation-based, and you’re currently having steady traffic of recommendations. Search-based audience or nonexistent recommendations traffic does not follow the same rule. In this case, experimentation is good, since you have nothing to lose!

YouTube algorithm isn’t a charity; once you get something, you have to give something in return. Once you earn its trust, better not break it.

Thank you for reading my article!

Feel free to comment down below and share your feedback, point of view, or your experience. Discussion is highly encouraged.

items.title: This Solo-Built Search Engine Is Challenging Big Tech
items.author: Chief Searcha
items.content:
It all started with a dream. Not a metaphorical one, but a literal, vivid dream about the early, wild west days of the internet. I woke up with a mission: to build a search engine that could rediscover the forgotten, personal webpages of that era. That dream, however, collided with the reality of the modern web.

What began as a nostalgic quest to revive the quirky, hand-coded corners of the internet evolved into something far more ambitious. I'm Ryan Pearce, and I'm the solo developer behind Searcha Co. From my home in Roseville, California, I've single-handedly built an independent, AI-enhanced search engine that now powers two public search sites:  and . And I did it all without a dollar of VC funding.

This is the story of how a one-person startup, armed with a shoestring budget and a lot of late nights, is taking on the giants of search.

The Underdog Ethos: Building a Search Engine from Scratch

After years in the corporate tech scene in Austin, I moved back to my hometown of Roseville, seeking a quieter life closer to family and the Sierra Nevada mountains. But the itch to build remained. The initial dream of a "nostalgia" search engine quickly ran into the harsh realities of the modern web - a landscape dominated by SEO spam and auto-generated content.

So, I pivoted. The new goal was to create a truly independent search engine, free from the influence of Big Tech APIs. This meant building everything from the ground up: the web crawler, the index, the ranking algorithms, and the user interface.

The Laundry Room Data Center

My initial setup was humble: a second-hand EPYC 7532 workstation with 512 GB of RAM and 40 TB of SSD storage, running out of my bedroom. The constant heat and fan noise quickly became unsustainable. The solution? I rerouted the entire system into my laundry closet, mounting the servers on wall shelves behind the door. It's a testament to the "whatever it takes" mentality of a boot-strapped founder.

This "laundry room data center," assembled with about $4,000 worth of hardware, now powers a search index of over two billion pages and can handle an estimated 50,000 searches a day.

A Tale of Two Search Sites: Privacy vs. Personalization

From this homegrown infrastructure, two distinct search experiences emerged, each with a different philosophy on privacy:

  •  This is for the privacy purists. It operates in a strict, stateless mode with no cookies, no user IDs, and no search history. It's designed for maximum anonymity.
  •  This site offers a more personalized experience. It uses a long-lived, browser-stored session to improve the relevance of search results over time. It's important to note that this is not tied to a personal account or IP address and does not profile users across the web.

This dual approach allows users to choose the level of privacy that they're comfortable with, a choice rarely offered by the dominant players in the search market.

The Power of AI in a One-Person Startup

Building a search engine solo would have been nearly impossible just a few years ago. Modern machine learning and AI-assisted development tools have leveled the playing field. I've leveraged Large Language Models (LLMs) for everything from query understanding and context linkage to ranking results.

But AI is not a silver bullet. My approach is a hybrid one, blending the power of AI with traditional search ranking methods to avoid hallucinations and ensure the results are reliable. In many real-world tests, the quality of the results is highly competitive with Google.

Beyond the search pipeline itself, I used AI to build my own internal software stack. This includes a custom-built task manager, CRM, analytics platform, and more. This "personal SaaS" has allowed me to operate as a solo founder without relying on expensive off-the-shelf software.

The Journey Ahead: A Call for Beta Testers

The launch of Seek Ninja and Searcha Page is just the beginning. I'm proud to be building this project in my hometown of Roseville and have long-term goals of generating enough revenue to work on this full-time, hire local talent, and eventually build a headquarters here.

Both search sites are currently in open beta, and I'm actively seeking feedback from the community. I invite you to try them out and let me know what you think. Building an alternative to Big Tech is a monumental task, but it's a challenge I'm passionate about.

In a world where a handful of tech giants control so much of our online experience, creating a viable, independent alternative feels like a victory in itself. This is more than just a search engine; it's a statement that with determination and the right tools, a single person can still make a dent in the universe.

items.title: Building a Simple Spotify Trends Dashboard with React & Data Viz Libraries
items.author: Daria Filozop
items.content:
I am a Software Engineering student and a beginner developer. For now, I am actively experimenting with data visualization and learning new tools for it.

In this article, I want to share a small but useful project that demonstrates how to create a one-page data analytics app.

So, speaking shortly, here I’ll show a way of building a complete web project using:

    Previously, I’ve had some experience working with Flexmonster, so I was already familiar with it. Also, I was impressed by how fast it can work with , and has an intuitively understandable UI where reports can be easily done.

    For chart visualization, we’ll use Highcharts because they suggest a lot of types of charts and are so easy to combine with Flexmonster. 

    Maybe the easiest one to explain: I’ve just been using React in some projects and really enjoyed working with it. Also, it is quite a popular framework, which makes development faster and keeps code clean and understandable.

    \ Then it was time to choose the dataset. For me, this process was quite long. I wanted to find something interesting and well-known. So, one random day, while listening to music, it hit me — why not use a Spotify dataset? Everyone knows it, it’s full of data, and who doesn’t want to see info about how high on the charts their favourite musician is?

    As you may already understand, I will use the from Kaggle to demonstrate how users can upload and analyze their own CSV data.

    This article is presented in a tutorial format, making it easy to apply to your own projects. I genuinely hope it's helpful.

    Step 1: Integrating Flexmonster Pivot

    I hope your React projects are already prepared, because now it’s time to integrate it ith Flexmonster!

    In the beginning, you need to install Flexmonster CLI from npm:

    npm install -g flexmonster-cli

    \ To get Flexmonster, you should open your React project and run the following command:

    flexmonster add js-flexmonster

    \ After that, the flexmonster npm package will be downloaded to your .

    Also, you can get Flexmonster not only from the CLI, but also from CDN, npm, ZIP package, and WebJars. To get more information about it, you can read about integrating Flexmonster .

    As I’m using React in this project, I need to add a React Wrapper for Flexmonster, which can be done by using this command:

    npm install react-flexmonster

    Step 2: Importing all necessary modules

    Now that we have Flexmonster installed and ready, let’s build the main interface of our application using React.

    We need to import all necessary modules at the top of file:

    import React, { useRef, useState, useEffect } from "react";
import { Pivot } from "react-flexmonster";
import "flexmonster/flexmonster.css";
import "./App.css";

    \ The pivot component from react-flexmonster is what renders the interactive pivot table. We also import Flexmonster’s CSS files to apply the default styling, and optionally, you can import your own custom styles with App.css.

    Step 3: Creating the Report Object

    Inside our App.jsx file, we create a report object that describes the data source, field mapping, layout, and visual options for the pivot table. 

    const report = {
    dataSource: {
        filename: "/spotify-top-200-dataset.csv",
        mapping: {
            artist_name: { type: "string" },
            track_name: { type: "string" },
            streams: { type: "number" },
        },
    },

    It’s important to mention the filename – path to the file (CSV file, in my case), it is spotify-top-200-dataset.csv.

    Step 4: Configuring the Pivot Table Slice

    Then we need to write the slice section, which defines how the data is grouped and summarized.

    I was thinking what info is the best to display and decided to choose the artist, tracks, and number of streams.

     So, to display it all, we need:

    • to include artists and track names in the rows; 
    • to show the total number of streams as measures; 
    • to apply sorting in descending order based on the stream count.
      slice: {
      rows: [
          { uniqueName: "artist_name" },
          { uniqueName: "track_name" }
      ],
      measures: [
          {
              uniqueName: "streams",
              aggregation: "sum" 
          }
          ],
      sorting: {
          column: {
              type: "desc",
              tuple: [],
              measure: "streams",
          },
      },
  },

    Now, we define the pivot grid component and add it to the page:

    return (
    <div id="app-container">
        <header>
            <h1>Spotify Insights</h1>
        </header>
        <main>
                <Pivot
                    key="original"
                    ref={pivotRef}
                    toolbar={true}
                    beforetoolbarcreated={customizeToolbar}
                    report={report}
                    licenseFilePath="https://cdn.flexmonster.com/jsfiddle.charts.key"
                    width="100%"
                    height="400px"
                />
            <div id="highcharts-container"></div>
        </main>
    </div>
);

    \ Here we define our pivot table, enabling the toolbar to be shown when we render the page. There is also an option to hide the toolbar, just don’t write the line

    Now, I suggest you make our project more interactive by customizing the toolbar. Being more precise, adding a new tab. One of the biggest advantages of pivot tables is that you can see all your data from different angles. In my case, I didn’t want to change “Tracks View” anymore, so this tab will help us, because we will create a completely new view which will demonstrate even more possibilities of pivot tables.

    For this, we need to create a function :

    function customizeToolbar(toolbar) {
    const tabs = toolbar.getTabs();
    toolbar.getTabs = function () {
        tabs.unshift({
            id: "fm-tab-newtab",
            title: showOriginalPivot ? "Albums" : "Tracks",
            handler: newTabHandler,
            icon: this.icons.options,
        });
        return tabs;
    };
}
    • Get all already existing tabs using
    • Add a new tab that will switch between two views (in our case, it will be two pivot tables)

    Step 7: Creating a Second Pivot Table

    For the second view, we will create a pivot table with albums. We should do the same steps as we did before but just a little bit change the data. For example, in report object we will display the year of release date, album name, artist name, and number of streams: 

    const genresReport = {
    dataSource: {
        filename: "/spotify-top-200-dataset.csv",
        mapping: {
            artist_name: { type: "string" },
            streams: { type: "number" },
            album_name: { type: "string" },
            release_date: { type: "date" }
        },
    },
    slice: {
        rows: [
            { uniqueName: "release_date.Year" },
            { uniqueName: "album_name" }
        ],
        columns: [
            {
                uniqueName: "artist_name",
                filter: {
                    measure: "streams",
                    type: "top",
                    quantity: 20
                },
                sort: "desc"
            }
        ],
        measures: [
            { uniqueName: "streams", aggregation: "sum" }
        ],
        sorting: {
            column: {
                type: "desc",
                tuple: [],
                measure: "streams"
            }
        }
    }
};

    Step 8: Install Highcharts and Bind Flexmonster to It

    To install Highcharts, write the following command in the terminal:

    Then, import Highcharts into your project:

    import Highcharts from "highcharts";
import "flexmonster/lib/flexmonster.highcharts.js";

    \ More about the integration of Flexmonster with Highcharts can be found .

    Inside your App.jsx, use useRef and useEffect to connect the pivot table with Highcharts:

    const pivotRef = useRef(null);

    useEffect(() => {
    const handleReportComplete = (): void => {
        const pivot = pivotRef.current?.flexmonster;

        if (pivot) {
            pivot.highcharts.getData(
                {
                    type: "column",
                },
                (data: GetDataValueObject): void => {
                    Highcharts.chart("highcharts-container", data);
                }
            );
        }
    };

    const pivot = pivotRef.current?.flexmonster;
    if (pivot) {
        pivot.on("reportcomplete", handleReportComplete);
    }
}, []);

    Here, pivotRef lets us get access to the pivot table instance so we can work with its built-in functions.

    We use the “reportcomplete” event to run our code once the pivot table has finished loading.

    Step 9: Displaying the Chart

    Once data is passed to Highcharts via pivot.highcharts.getData(…), the chart will automatically appear in the container with the specified chart type. You can use any chart type supported by Highcharts, for example line, pie, or bar, by changing the type passed to getData.

    I сhose to use a column chart for this project. It's a familiar and visually intuitive way to display data. You can enable it in your code by specifying:

    pivot.highcharts.getData({ type: "column" }, (data) => {
    with a proper id for the Highcharts component:
    <div id="highcharts-container"></div>

    \ When the Flexmonster pivot table finishes loading, reportcomplete event is triggered. Then, we extract data using Flexmonster’s Highcharts integration and render it.

    Step 10: Customizing your web app

    And here is my favorite part of every project - styling and customizing!

    I tried to make these grids as similar to Spotify's original design as possible: font, black background, and green color. 

    \ \ So, how to implement it all? Let’s separately talk about Flexmonster pivot table and Highcharts visualizations.

    Step 10.1: Customizing Flexmonster

    Flexmonster has built-in themes, and all possible options are available in this .

    However, as we want to do it in Spotify style, as much as possible, we’ll need to create a custom theme.

    Firstly, you need to run these commands in your terminal to get theme-builder:

    git clone https://github.com/flexmonster/custom-theme-builder
cd custom-theme-builder

    \ In the c folder, we should find and open the file. There you can apply any styles you want.

    After it, we need to compile file into , which can be easily done with the following command:

    lessc flexmonster.less flexmonster.css

    Then, add file to your project folder.

    In index.html add this line:

    <link rel="stylesheet" href="/flexmonster.css" />

    \ And also don’t forget to import flexmonster.css file to your project in 

    import "flexmonster/flexmonster.css";
import "../flexmonster.css"

    \ When you complete all these steps, you can already see a customized pivot table in your own style!

    Step 10.2: Customizing Highcharts

    Unfortunately, not all the CSS code can be applied to Highcharts, so to reduce the number of bugs and difficulties, I decided to do the styling directly in App.jsx. 

    Here is the code with already implemented styling of Highcharts:

    const handleReportComplete = () => {
    pivot.highcharts.getData({ type: "column" }, (data) => {
        Highcharts.chart("highcharts-container", {
            ...data,
            chart: {
                ...data.chart,
                backgroundColor: "transparent",
                style: {
                    fontFamily:
                        "'Poppins', 'Circular Spotify Text', 'Helvetica Neue', Helvetica, Arial, sans-serif",
                },
            },
            title: {
                text: "Spotify Top 200 Streams",
                style: {
                    color: "#1DB954",
                    fontWeight: "900",
                    fontSize: "26px",
                    textShadow: "0 0 8px #1DB954",
                },
            },
            xAxis: {
                ...data.xAxis,
                labels: {
                    style: { color: "#E3E3E3", fontSize: "13px" },
                },
                lineColor: "#1DB954",
                tickColor: "#1DB954",
            },
            yAxis: {
                ...data.yAxis,
                labels: {
                    style: { color: "#E3E3E3", fontSize: "13px" },
                },
                gridLineColor: "rgba(29, 185, 84, 0.3)",
                title: {
                    text: "Streams",
                    style: { color: "#1DB954", fontWeight: "700" },
                },
            },
            legend: {
                enabled: false,
            },
            plotOptions: {
                column: {
                    color: "#1DB954",
                    borderRadius: 6,
                    shadow: {
                        color: "rgba(29, 185, 84, 0.8)",
                        offsetX: 0,
                        offsetY: 0,
                        opacity: 0.9,
                        width: 12,
                    },
                },
            },
            tooltip: {
                backgroundColor: "#1DB954",
                style: { color: "#121212", fontWeight: "700" },
                borderRadius: 8,
                borderWidth: 0,
                shadow: true,
                animation: true,
            },
            series: data.series,
        });
    });
};

    \ Feel free to change it to the way that works best for you!

    Finally, we have our project completely done, and I hope that you are also happy with the result!

    Also, I’d love to hear your feedback and any thoughts about this webpage.

    Thanks for reading, and good luck with coding!

items.title: Is AI the End of Sustainability?
items.author: Kateryna Korotieieva
items.content:
\ When people hear “sustainability,” they often jump to carbon emissions and climate goals. But in product management, sustainability isn’t just “let’s go green”. It’s part of the strategy. It’s about building products that endure, teams that don’t burn out, and practices that scale.

Sustainable product management means leading with longevity in mind, placing team resilience at the core, and choosing strategic clarity over feature churn. Oftentimes, the most burned-out teams aren’t doing too much - they are doing too much without clarity.

Let’s break down what that actually looks like in the age of AI.

\ Four Dimensions of Product Sustainability in the AI Era

1. Data-Driven Sustainability

Good AI is only as good as its data. Product teams must prioritize data quality over quantity: de-biased, ethically sourced, and usable in real-world scenarios. The PM’s role is to ensure that AI-powered features are built on solid foundations. That means left-shifting data validation early in discovery, asking the right questions before models are trained, and aligning data pipelines with ethical and legal standards.

As Marty Cagan, the founder of SVPG, highlights, early “expert system” AI failed because it couldn’t adapt beyond rigid rules. Real intelligence depends on probability, fresh data, and continuous learning.

==According to McKinsey, organizations that effectively use high‑quality analytics report up to a 20 % increase in customer satisfaction and a 15 % bump in revenue==, showing how data-driven decisions can sustainably improve outcomes. That’s what sustainable product thinking is about.

AI should solve problems, not create noise. Chasing flashy use cases might yield short-term gains, but true product sustainability means serving real, long-term user needs. PMs must guard against “AI for AI’s sake.” Instead, they should double down on using AI to reduce friction, improve accessibility, and offer enduring differentiation. Sustainable products don’t inflate KPIs at the expense of trust or UX, but instead, they earn user loyalty over time. Marty Cagan calls this an “intelligent product” — AI is not just a bolted-on feature, but “a key to intelligent products.” Other respected product specialists, like Aakash Gupta, echo this too: “The most powerful AI implementations are the ones users barely notice.”

When AI is baked into the core solution and not just sprinkled on, the value to the customer endures, giving companies stronger positioning that doesn’t age out in six months. It’s a reminder that long-term market sustainability depends on building solutions where AI quietly does the real work, not just decorates the pitch deck.

3. Product Management Sustainability (Team & Role)

AI changes how we build, but it doesn’t change what great product management looks like. If anything, it raises the bar. PMs today must think beyond tasks and timelines. Strategic thinking, relentless curiosity, and cross-functional collaboration are non-negotiable. AI should be treated as a decision-support tool, not an autonomous product brain.

In this case, success is about asking the right questions, connecting the dots, and keeping the user at the center.

==Success = strategic thinking + cross-functional collaboration + product sense + communication.==

4. Organizational Sustainability

High performance shouldn’t come at the cost of team well-being. Sustainable orgs structure their product practices for clarity, not chaos. That means layered roadmaps that show both business intent and technical realities. It means empowering product owners to own and maintain clean backlogs. And it means introducing strategy rituals, not just execution standups, to keep everyone aligned.

When vision, process, and context are shared across roles, teams don’t just ship faster but also thrive longer.

\ Why AI Demands More Sustainable Thinking (Not Less)

Ironically, AI often increases ambiguity. Model drift, degraded data, and black-box behavior introduce new long-term risks. PMs must step in as the structure-bringers. That means building for explainability, planning for retraining, and budgeting for maintenance. Transparency and human oversight are the backbone of trustworthy AI.

Sustainable thinking isn’t just a “nice to have” - it’s how AI products avoid becoming tech debt within a year of launch.

\ A Sustainable AI Product Lifecycle: 5 Questions PMs Should Ask

  1. Is this product solving a problem that will still matter in 12–24 months?
  2. Is our data pipeline maintainable and ethical?
  3. Are we training our teams to understand (not just ship) AI-driven features?
  4. Do we have a plan for scaling usage and model governance?
  5. Will users trust this over time, not just try it once? \n

Final Thoughts: Product Managers as Stewards of Longevity

Sustainability in product isn’t about slowing down - it’s about getting sharper. It’s about designing teams, processes, and features that don’t just launch fast but last. And in the age of AI, that mindset is more vital than ever. PMs are the ones who can shape this shift. Not by doing everything, but by asking better questions, clarifying purpose, and protecting what matters most: the long-term health of our products, our users, and our teams.

items.title: Best VPS & VDS Hosting 2025: The Only Guide You’ll Need for Smart, Scalable Projects
items.author: Dorian Zorn
items.content:
Have you ever wondered why some projects grow quickly and steadily while others stall in the same place for years? Often, the reason isn’t the idea or the team — it’s the infrastructure. Choosing the right virtual server is what allows a business to run smoothly, handle traffic spikes, and avoid costly downtime.

In 2025, the hosting market offers hundreds of solutions, each boasting impressive features on paper. But if you look closer, behind the marketing and discounts lie critical details that can make or break your project: where your data is stored, how well your site handles traffic surges, and who will actually help if something breaks in the middle of the night.

This article is for those who want to make informed decisions, invest in growth, and stay one step ahead of the competition. We’ve gathered only what truly impacts results: how to choose VPS or VDS tailored to your needs, what specs to focus on, who to trust, and how to avoid hidden fees.

Everything below is a concentrated distillation of real-world experience and practical knowledge. If you’re ready to work for results, move fast, and never settle for less — let’s get started.

My House. My Rules. My Coffee.

Let’s start with the basics.

Most projects kick off on shared hosting. It’s fast, simple, and cheap: you get some server space, access via a control panel, and a standard set of features. But here’s the catch — all your resources (CPU, RAM, storage) are shared with dozens, sometimes hundreds, of other sites. If your neighbor runs a heavy task or hits an error, it hits your project too.

Need an analogy? Remember the movie Parasite, where the family lives in a semi-basement: cramped, little air, little light, and if the neighbor floods, the water floods you too.

A VPS (Virtual Private Server) is a whole different league. Here, you get your own “apartment” inside a shared building: a separate operating system, dedicated resources, and full control over configurations. Want to update software, install custom packages, or set up your own proxy? Nobody’s stopping you.

I often explain this transition through pop culture: shared hosting is like living in that basement with constant restrictions, while VPS is your own studio apartment where you’re the boss.

I was fascinated by the movie Knives Out — there’s a scene where Marta Cabrera stands on a balcony holding a mug that says: “My house, my rules, my coffee.”

Viewed through this lens, that’s the essence of VPS: your rules, your decisions, your infrastructure.

But the difference between shared hosting and VPS isn’t just a feeling. Behind the fancy slogans lie real business risks. Staying on shared hosting is like running a sprint hoping for a marathon win. It might save you money in the short term, but it risks your growth.

Looking at company experiences, auditor data, and real case studies, I can say plainly: slow and unreliable sites lose customers — and profits. The stats confirm it — 80% of users won’t come back if a site “lags.”

The VPS market is booming, growing at 12–15% annually, with a total volume nearing $8 billion and rising.

Shared hosting still works for simple tasks: trial projects, personal pages, MVPs. But when it comes to business, traffic, and revenue — the demands change.

Every extra second of delay means lost money: Amazon reportedly loses up to $1.6 billion annually just from slow loading times. For small businesses, the losses might be less obvious but just as critical for survival.

If you notice your traffic growing, marketing budgets expanding, and conversion rates becoming crucial — shared hosting won’t cut it anymore. Why? Because dependency on your neighbors, resource limits, and lack of flexibility kill your chances to grow.

Switching to VPS/VDS is a conscious investment: dedicated resources, scaling on demand, real support, and guarantees. It’s the foundation on which sustainable projects are built.

Shared Hosting vs VPS: What’s Really Different:

| | | VPS (Virtual Private Server) | |----|----|----| | | Only basic panel, no root access | Full root/admin access, own OS | | | None, all resources shared | Partial: resources allocated, some soft limits | | | Depends on neighbors, can degrade unpredictably | More stable, resources usually reserved | | | Weakest: neighbor’s problem = your problem | Stronger: separate environments | | | Minimal (fixed stack, standard templates) | Full freedom (install anything, configure deeply) | | | Very limited | Medium to high, easy to scale up/down | | | Blogs, landing pages, MVP, low-stakes projects | Growing business, SaaS, e-commerce, side projects | | | Lowest, sometimes even free | Moderate: higher, but justified by benefits | | Support Offered (by Provider) | Minimal (self-service, slow tickets) | Faster, often 24/7, but deep help paid extra | | | Minimal (no admin skills) | Intermediate to advanced (Linux, admin, etc.) | | | “Noisy neighbor effect”, downtime, random crashes | Self-management required: higher responsibility for setup, updates, and security. If something breaks or gets hacked — you’re on your own. |

\n Money Talks: How to Size Up a VPS Like a Pro

When choosing a VPS for your business, it doesn’t matter how flashy the marketing sounds — what matters is that the server actually fits your needs and drives revenue, not headaches. Everything else is just details.

  1. — Trust actions, not words Don’t just look at the number of CPU cores and GBs of RAM on paper. Check the real guarantees: what resources are actually allocated, the provider’s overselling policy, and whether there are load restrictions in the SLA. It’s better to have fewer resources officially guaranteed but no slowdowns during peak times. I recommend reading reviews not only on the provider’s site but also on independent platforms — real-world experience beats marketing every time.

    A provider promises 4 cores and 8 GB RAM, but the server crashes under the first mass email blast — your site slows down, some users can’t place orders, CRM glitches, and the business loses money for no reason.

    A reputable provider transparently specifies guaranteed resources and offers a trial period. You run stress tests — everything holds steady even during peak hours. The marketing promise pays off instead of turning into losses.

  2. — SSD, NVMe, not “archival” HDDs By 2025, this is no longer a luxury but a baseline for any traffic-heavy project. The faster the storage system, the lower the latency, and the higher the conversion and customer loyalty.

    For e-commerce and SaaS, not only speed but also database stability is critical.

    Your site “lags” on every load, the database occasionally freezes, and users spend 3–5 seconds waiting for pages to open. For an online store, this can mean losing up to 50% of potential customers.

    With SSD/NVMe, the site flies even under increased traffic. Users pay before their patience runs out — and leave happy.

Location & Scalability: Growth Without Limits

  1. Geolocation — A five-minute round trip adventure

    The closer your server is to your target audience, the faster your site or app responds. For Europe and the US, data centers within the region are essential. For global services, multiple locations are a must. Sometimes it’s worth paying a little extra for better speed and lower latency.

    You launch a service for European users, but your server is in Singapore. The result? Delays, lower search rankings, and a poor user experience.

    Your server is in the right region, loading is fast, and SEO rankings are high. Customers don’t notice the technical details — they just get a smooth, instant service.

  2. SLA & Support — Your response team

    A solid SLA and fast support are like insurance: you don’t notice them while everything’s fine, but they save your business when things go south.

    The best providers go beyond promises: if they breach the SLA, you may receive compensation—not just for downtime itself, but sometimes even for your business losses during the outage. Of course, such guarantees usually come at a premium, but for mission-critical projects, this level of commitment is worth the investment.

    Check how quickly tickets get answered and whether support channels actually work during nights and weekends.

    A provider without live support is a risk your business can’t afford.

    At the worst possible moment, your database crashes, and support just replies with “Your ticket has been received.” Meanwhile, you lose clients and revenue.

    A live operator responds within 10 minutes and fixes the issue before your customers even notice.

Support & Transparency: No Room for Surprises

  1. Pricing Transparency — Oh no, what’s with the fine print?

    Carefully review the pricing terms: hidden traffic limits, paid backups, extra fees for migration or upgrades are common traps. A good provider lays everything out upfront; a bad one hides details until the bill arrives.

    Everything looks great “on promotion” at first, but then unexpected charges for traffic or backups hit your budget hard, making financial planning impossible.

    All conditions are clear from the start, no hidden fees. Your business can confidently calculate ROI without unpleasant surprises from the provider.

  2. Flexibility & Scalability

    You need one server today — tomorrow three — and next month back to one. It’s crucial that your provider allows easy scaling up or down without long waits, excessive fees, or complicated migrations.

    During peak sales, your server can’t handle the load, and upgrades take weeks. Potential orders are lost.

    You log in to the control panel, click a few buttons — resources increase instantly. Everything runs smoothly, profits grow.

    Choosing a VPS is not a lottery. True pros look not for “maximum specs for pennies,” but for a service that can handle business load any day. Don’t fall for flashy packaging — demand what truly works for your goals and growth.

Pick Your Poison: Hosting Use Cases That Actually Matter

Choosing a hosting provider isn’t a “one-and-done” decision — it’s about matching your choice to specific goals, business models, growth pace, and pain points. In this section, we’ll break down the key scenarios almost every project builder or scaler encounters in 2025.

For small teams, early-stage startups, MVPs, hobby projects, quick idea validation, and “late-night launches” on a tight budget, the key factors are flexibility, low entry barriers, and the ability to experiment without consequences. You don’t need gigabytes of RAM or 32-core processors here — what matters most is the ability to quickly deploy your project, switch plans without red tape, and create backups with just a couple of clicks.

VPS has become the gold standard for anyone unwilling to pay for air: you start with the simplest plan, monitor user behavior, and scale resources as needed — without overspending. It’s not just about price; “speed to market” is critical: the less hassle with setup and upgrades, the faster your idea hits the market.

You launch a SaaS product for remote teams or a pet project with growth potential. In the first month, you need to support 100 users; by month two, that number grows to a thousand. Shared hosting will collapse at the first iteration — no flexibility, no stability, no real support. VPS lets you flexibly increase limits, test new features, deploy beta versions on separate instances — and not fear that a traffic spike will crash the entire system.

A designer or developer creates an MVP demo for investors. They launch, present, get feedback, and within 10 minutes change configs or restore a backup — all simple and fast, no need to call support and wait for a miracle.

For online stores, marketplaces, and services where uptime and speed are paramount, the key factors are predictability, stability, and the protection of transactions and personal data.

In these areas, every second of downtime or delay isn’t just an inconvenience — it’s a direct financial loss. Customers won’t wait around while a page “thinks” about loading — they’ll leave for a site that runs smoothly.

Advanced VPS with strong SLA guarantees (or even VDS/dedicated servers when the load approaches enterprise levels) wins here.

You need true resource isolation, dedicated IP addresses, built-in backup solutions, and the ability to instantly scale resources during traffic surges.

Seasonal sales (Black Friday, holiday promotions) cause an instant spike in concurrent visitors. On shared hosting, the site crashes, losing dozens or even hundreds of orders per minute. With VPS, you can preemptively increase limits or spin up additional instances — handling the full customer load without failures.

Integrations with payment systems and external services mean even brief outages lead to “stuck” payments, corrupted analytics, and increased chargebacks.

Protecting personal and payment data is a must in e-commerce. A good VPS lets you implement your own encryption, customize firewalls, and rely on your provider for quick rollback and data recovery if something goes wrong.

A global electronics e-commerce platform (let’s call it “TechX”) switched from shared hosting to VPS after their site crashed on Cyber Monday due to heavy traffic — losing $50,000 in unfulfilled orders in a single day. The next year, with VPS and flexible limit management, there wasn’t a single lost order, and the company’s reputation improved.

In e-commerce and mission-critical projects, there are no “small details”: infrastructure is the backbone of your business, and even brief downtime can cost more than an entire year’s worth of hosting savings. \n \n Security-First & Scaling Up

If your project deals with sensitive personal data, fintech, or B2B solutions where even the slightest leak means disaster, “budget” shared hosting is simply not an option.

Here, VPS — and for the most stringent requirements, dedicated servers — aren’t just nice-to-haves; they are regulatory and market necessities.

You get a true “sandbox”: the ability to build isolated environments for each client or internal service, create separate VLANs, configure network policies, manage backups and encryption, and control physical data locations.

  • Compliance with standards like GDPR (Europe), CCPA, HIPAA (USA), PCI DSS (payment data) — all require full infrastructure control, auditing capabilities, logging, and data protection at every stage. \n
  • Rapid scalability: as your business grows, you need to instantly add resources for new services or regions without changing the base architecture. \n
  • Enterprise-grade security: two-factor authentication, separate encryption keys, firewalls, IDS/IPS, isolated backups across data centers, and automatic alerts on intrusion attempts.

A fintech startup moves from shared hosting to VPS: the team not only meets GDPR and local regulatory requirements but can also automate backups, integrate third-party monitoring solutions, quickly isolate suspicious traffic, and most importantly — guarantee clients that their data is protected not just “on paper,” but in reality.

On shared hosting or “cheap” VPS without guaranteed isolation, one client’s vulnerability can threaten all — from simple DDoS attacks to complex cross-account hacks.

A VPS with proper isolation and configuration mitigates most of these risks, and when needed, lets you scale up to your own private cloud: adding instances, integrating threat detection systems, and storing data in different regions to comply with laws.

For businesses where money, data, and reputation are on the line, infrastructure must be fully controllable and flexible.

Choosing the right VPS means not just “one-click” scaling but building the security foundation that separates a trustworthy project from just another “disposable” platform.

In a world where every project is a bet, and every second of downtime can cost as much as Jordan Belfort’s new car, infrastructure is not just an expense — it’s your main asset and your insurance against failure.

At the MVP stage, you can still take a chance on shared hosting, but if you’re aiming to play big, VPS is the only scenario where you call the shots.

In any segment — whether startup, e-commerce, or fintech — speed, reliability, and control determine who “makes the market” and who stays a spectator.

But just choosing a server type isn’t enough. The winners are not those who trust marketing pages but those who actually test every provider in the field.

The most interesting part is just beginning: it’s time to find out who’s really playing in the big leagues, who stands strong in critical moments, and whose support is your business’s safety net.

Provider Showdown: Who’s Really Got Your Back in 2025?

It’s probably no secret to you that the market is flooded with offers. Everyone’s trying to sell you something, promising the best prices, NVMe drives, and 99.99% uptime.

But unfortunately, reality is different: some providers crumble under load, others feed you ticket responses, and some “support teams” disappear when the server catches fire.

So, I’ve taken the liberty to compile a list of trusted providers for you. This is not an ad — I’ll back up every choice with forum reviews and other reliable sources.

Speed & Value: Who Sets the Pace

Why start with speed and price-to-performance ratio? Because even the smartest service is useless if your site or database “dies” during prime time, or an upgrade leads to three days of downtime.

In this game, winners are those who keep their promises: who don’t just talk but truly handle peak loads, enable rapid scaling, and don’t drive you crazy with upgrades and pricing.

All the providers below are names you’ll find consistently mentioned on forums — each has been “discussed” by hundreds of project owners, not just marketers.

Founded in 1997, Hetzner is the workhorse for SaaS and any projects where price/performance ratio matters. German precision without compromises.

Independent tests show that even on the lowest plan you get NVMe drives, fast CPUs (often EPYC or Xeon), 1–10 Gbit network, and reliably transparent billing.

  • NVMe storage, transparent pricing, user-friendly control panel, automation, high availability (data centers in Germany and Finland meet Tier III+ and ISO 27001 standards).
  • Support isn’t beginner-friendly, communication mostly in English/German, occasional queues for new server deployments.

Hetzner’s large customer base and competitive pricing mean their abuse team enforces strict policies—so be sure to review their rules carefully, as violations may result in account restrictions.

  • “Faster than DigitalOcean for less money. No data loss in two years, and the panel after AWS is just bliss.”

The second on the list is a European giant known for SLA, automation, extensive data center network, and strong DDoS protection. Founded in 1999.

Clients note real uptime matches advertised 99.97%–99.99%, and infrastructure recovers quickly from failures.

  • SLA, inter-data-center network, “steel-reinforced” security, automation API, vast product range from bare metal to public cloud.
  • Support may be slower than Hetzner or UpCloud; billing requires occasional attention.
  • “Handles traffic spikes like a champ, DDoS doesn’t faze it at all. Almost a standard for big stores.”

Best value for starting out or projects needing “lots for little.” Founded in 2003.

In 2024, Contabo upgraded its platform, adding NVMe drives to all plans while keeping some of the lowest prices around. Forums warn: if your project grows, plan resource buffers in advance.

  • Huge RAM and SSD/NVMe volumes compared to competitors, transparent pricing, fast onboarding.
  • Under heavy load, disks slower than Hetzner or UpCloud; support not instant.
  • “RAM tests show dedicated memory, but under load I/O drops a bit — still a workhorse for media and archives at this price.”

“Maximum speed” isn’t just a slogan: their proprietary MaxIOPS technology offers minimal latency even under heavy disk operation. Founded in 2012.

Popular for businesses where milliseconds count: brokers, fintech, gaming projects, real-time APIs.

  • MaxIOPS tech, fast CPUs, minimal latency, geo-location options (Finland, Germany, Singapore, USA, UK), flexible panel and API.
  • Price above average, but pays off with consistent load.
  • “API faster than DO and Linode, one of the best panels. If latency is critical — this is it.”

French engineering open to experimentation: ARM instances, non-standard pricing, eco-friendly data centers, and proprietary S3-compatible storage.

For those who love trying new things, building on ARM, or running custom environments.

The youngest on this list, founded in 2015.

  • ARM support, eco-conscious, innovative, flexible pricing, modern UI.
  • Not suited for critical e-commerce or high-load projects, occasional bugs in new services.
  • “Built my pet cloud on ARM for pennies. Cool ecosystem, but for serious business I’d pick Hetzner or OVH.”

Speed & Value Comparison:

| | | | | |----|----|----|----| | | €3.79 (CX Intel) / €4.35 (CPX AMD) | NVMe, fair pricing, Tier III+, ISO 27001 | Support not for beginners | | | €5.50 for VPS | SLA 99.9%, DDoS protection, global network, API | Support can be slow \n | | | from €4.55/month for NVMe VPS | Maximum RAM and disk for the price | I/O bottlenecks under load | | | from €3/month for public cloud | MaxIOPS, minimal latency, 99.999% SLA | Cost above mid-tier segment | | | from €31/month for ARM | ARM instances, energy efficiency, customization | Not for high loads; beginner UX |

  • offers NVMe options starting from €3.79 for Intel and €4.35 for AMD, featuring powerful processors and strong reliability.
  • starts at around €5.50 per VPS, delivering up to 99.99% SLA, DDoS protection, and API access.
  • provides NVMe VPS from €3.00 to €4.50, making it an excellent choice for projects that need lots of RAM and disk space.
  • offers public cloud VPS from $3.50–$4 per month with a high SLA and MaxIOPS technology.
  • ARM-based instances now start from €0.0426/hour (about €31/month), reflecting recent changes in their lineup. While they still offer ARM and S3-compatible storage, the ultra-budget ARM options from previous years are no longer available.

Support That’s Actually There

If speed and resources are the engine of any project, then support and real people are your insurance policy and emergency response team—the safety net that keeps even the best server from crashing when it matters most.

Speed is only half the battle. The moment something goes wrong, all those impressive numbers become meaningless if support doesn’t respond or just sends you copy-paste replies.

The real question isn’t problems will arise, but —and how quickly you’ll get the help you need.

  1. One of the oldest Western players, having outlasted many waves of competition. The largest hosting provider in Europe by number of clients and projects. Founded in 1988.

    : 24/7 support, rapid ticket response (up to 15 minutes, according to forums), dedicated business lines, flexible VPS/VDS range.

    : Sometimes you’ll get generic responses from first-line support, and the control panel can feel dated.

    : “They literally saved my project—restored a backup at night, and I was back online in 20 minutes.” “Moved from Bluehost—support here is on a whole different level.”

  2. An international cloud provider with data centers worldwide, often cited as a benchmark for customization and personal approach. Founded in 1995.

    : Personalized support—consultants really help, you can reach actual engineers, 30-day free trial, instant resource scaling.

    : The control panel is unusual for those coming from classic providers like DO/Hetzner, price is above average.

    : “Two years with Kamatera—never had an incident without a response, they even called my cell when a service went down.” “Perfect for complex projects, never get template responses, even at night.”

  3. A veteran of the US market, with a focus on client service and transparency. Founded in 1996.

    : Live support chat, honest answers (often acknowledge issues right away), instant ticket creation, free website migration.

    : Phone support is paid, chat may be unavailable at night (US time).

    : “Moved after struggling with GoDaddy—here, they fixed a bug in 30 minutes.” “Almost never got bounced between agents, they actually respect the client.”

  4. Known not just for domain registration, but also for quality VPS/hosting support. Founded in 2000.

    : 24/7 chat and ticket support, fast reaction to incidents, free project migration, solid knowledge base.

    : Sometimes “juniors” on first-line support, slower response times at night.

    : “Migrated my project at night—support didn’t go to bed, walked me through to the end.” “Sometimes reply with scripts, but if you push, they escalate to an engineer.”

  5. A young European provider with a small team, focused on personal contact and minimal bureaucracy. Founded in 2015.

    : Direct access to engineers, real dialogue, no “ticket ping-pong,” work nights & weekends.

    : Not always an instant response, but honest about timing; limited educational materials.

    : “Five stars for VPS and support. Sometimes slow to pick up, but always handle things professionally.” “Not a giant, but you can negotiate like humans, not scripts.”

    \ Support & Response Comparison:

| | Starting Price (per month) | | | |----|----|----|----| | | €4–6 | 24/7 chat, phone, email | ≤ 15 minutes | | | $4.00–$6.00 | 24/7 chat, tickets, phone | Minutes to hours | | | $5.99 | Chat, tickets | ~30 minutes | | | $3.88–$6.88 | 24/7 chat, tickets | Minutes to hours (slower at night) | | | €5.59+ | Tickets, Email | ≤ 1 hour |

Table Notes & Explanations:

  • is the top pick for those who want honest price-to-performance. Even the lowest plans offer NVMe drives and server-grade CPUs like EPYC or Xeon, more than enough for excellent speed and stability. The main trade-off: support is tailored for experienced users and might not be the friendliest for complete beginners. \n

  • leads in SLA guarantees and DDoS protection. It’s a go-to for large projects demanding rock-solid reliability and security. While their support can be slow at times, the sheer resilience of their infrastructure and vast data center network make up for it. If uptime is your top priority, OVHcloud is hard to beat. \n

  • is the choice for anyone who needs maximum RAM and storage at a budget-friendly price. Just be aware that disk speeds can drop under peak loads, so factor this in if your workload is storage-intensive. \n

  • stands out for latency-critical workloads. Their MaxIOPS tech and minimal latency make them a favorite for fintech and gaming projects. Prices are slightly above average, but the hardware and support quality justify the cost. \n

  • is the playground for those who like to experiment: ARM instances, eco-friendly data centers, and non-standard plans. A great pick for innovative or personal projects, but not ideal for heavy-traffic or mission-critical apps. \n

  • is a young, ambitious European cloud provider that pairs near-bare-metal speed and 99.99% uptime promises. Round-the-clock engineers answer tickets or emails in under an hour, so issues never stall your release schedule. If you’re after DevOps-ready flexibility and enterprise-grade reliability without enterprise prices, Peerobyte is built to power your next project. \n \n For Builders, Tinkerers, and Growth

    Let’s be honest—if you’re reading this, you’re probably not here for “just a website.” Maybe you’re running scripts at odd hours or you’ve got a half-broken side project running somewhere in the background (don’t worry, we’ve all been there). Infrastructure isn’t just a box you check off—sometimes it’s where all the real fun (or chaos) happens.

    Forget marketing promises and endless support chats. What you need is freedom—room to tinker, experiment, and sometimes break things before making them even better. APIs, custom setups, secret test environments… that’s the good stuff.

    The best platforms for builders? They stay out of your way when you’re in the flow, and show up fast when you break something big. This isn’t about “maximum stability”—it’s about keeping you moving, not holding you back.

    So if you’re that person who thinks, “Why not try it?” at 1 a.m., or you like having total control (even if it sometimes blows up), you’ll feel right at home here.

    One of the pioneers of simple, accessible cloud hosting, and a longtime favorite among startups and developers worldwide. Founded in 2011.

    Straightforward dashboard, thousands of tutorials, robust API, and a vibrant community. Perfect for rapid prototyping and scaling MVPs.

    : Simplicity, scalability, a wide selection of ready-to-go solutions.

    : Higher prices for basic resources, not always ideal for heavy workloads.

    : “Started with a $5 droplet, scaled up quickly with zero downtime, and the community is always there to help.”

    A developer-focused cloud classic, with transparent pricing and a user-friendly API. Founded in 2003.

    Excellent Linux support and fast app deployments.

    : Great value, reliability, and an active user base.

    : No Windows support, steeper learning curve for newcomers.

    : “Solid service with advanced configuration options—perfect for projects with real growth ambitions.” \n

    Focused on instant deployment and a vast selection of global data centers. Founded in 2014.

    A top pick for those who need granular control over location and flexible project scaling.

    : Fast setup, lots of regions, easy scalability.

    : Support can be limited, and ticket responses are sometimes slow.

    : “Great choice for startups with distributed audiences, but support could be better.”

    \ 4. Google Cloud Platform (USA)

    A true tech giant. As one of the Big Three in global cloud, GCP offers a massive suite of services for building scalable, flexible solutions. Founded in 2008.

    Ideal for projects aiming to grow into large-scale companies.

    : Massive scalability, powerful AI/analytics, global reach.

    : Complex for beginners, higher costs, demands solid admin skills.

    : “Best for serious projects ready to invest in DevOps and cloud infrastructure.”

    \ 5. Amazon Web Services (AWS) (USA)

    The largest, most feature-rich cloud provider in the world—the kingpin of the industry. Founded in 2006.

    Unmatched toolkit, flexible architecture, global data centers.

    : Unrivaled flexibility and scalability, endless services.

    : Steep learning curve, pricing can be confusing or unpredictable (watch out for surprise bills), expensive for small projects.

    : “Can’t imagine a scalable SaaS without AWS, but be prepared for a long learning journey.”

    \

    A major player in the global cloud market and a leader in hybrid and enterprise solutions. Founded in 2010.

    Best-in-class for seamless integration with Windows environments, Active Directory, and Microsoft SaaS products. Particularly strong in enterprise, government, and regulated industries.

    : Deep Windows integration, hybrid cloud, vast SaaS/PaaS ecosystem, strong compliance, global data centers, enterprise support.

    : Pricing can be complex, steep learning curve for advanced features, support is tiered by subscription.

    : “Indispensable for hybrid environments and enterprise Windows workflows. The go-to for building SaaS on Microsoft stack, but pricing clarity could be better.”

    Builders & Growth: Provider Comparison:

| | Starting Price (per month) | | | Avg. Support Response Time | |----|----|----|----|----| | | $4.00–$6.00 | Simple UI, rich API, large community | Startups, developers, MVPs | < 15 minutes (chat/tickets) \n | | | $5.00 | Transparent pricing, reliable, strong Linux support | Developers, SMBs | 15–30 minutes (chat/tickets) \n | | | $2.50–$5.00 | Fast deploy, global data centers, flexible | Startups, geo-distributed apps | 30–60 minutes (tickets) \n | | | Varies (free tier available) | Enterprise-grade, AI/ML, global infrastructure | Enterprises, scaleups | Minutes to hours (varies) \n | | | Varies (free tier available) | Most comprehensive, highly scalable | Enterprises, complex workloads | Minutes to hours (varies) | | | Varies (free tier available) | Windows integration, hybrid cloud, SaaS | Enterprises, hybrid, SaaS | Minutes to hours (varies) |

  • remains a favorite for fast MVP launches thanks to its simplicity and active community, but you’ll pay a bit more for basic resources compared to entry-level providers. \n
  • is known for reliability and strong Linux support, making it a solid choice for projects that expect steady growth. \n
  • attracts users with low entry prices and a massive network of data centers, though support response times can be less predictable. \n
  • are the industry heavyweights, offering an incredible range of services and truly scalable solutions. However, they require a higher level of expertise and a bigger budget, and support response times can vary depending on your plan. \n
  • is the leading platform for hybrid cloud, enterprise Windows environments, and deep SaaS integrations. Its unrivaled compatibility with Microsoft products, strong compliance features, and global infrastructure make it a top choice for corporate and government clients. The pricing model and learning curve can be steep, but for organizations already invested in the Microsoft ecosystem, Azure often delivers unmatched flexibility and scalability. \n

Conclusion: Who Wins Your Business?

Let’s be honest—there’s no “one size fits all” in hosting. Everything comes down to your real needs, not just glossy promises. Running a rubber ducky shop for your local community? Your priorities will be lightyears away from someone juggling carsharing fleets or processing financial data in real time.

is a magnet for anyone chasing solid European reliability without burning through their budget. It’s the no-nonsense pick for those who care about performance over packaging.

? That’s for the tinkerers and global players—the ones who need to spin up custom setups on three continents and get human support, fast, when things go sideways.

? Still the sweetheart for developers and early-stage teams. Simple, fast, and scalable—if your next app suddenly takes off, you won’t be left scrambling.

Here’s a tip you won’t hear in marketing copy: sometimes even the way providers lay out their pricing says a lot. Most will show you the cheapest plan first—“start small, dream big.” But I’ve seen a few turn things upside down, leading with the top-tier “dream” server. Subtle? Maybe. Memorable? Absolutely.

In the end, the right provider is the one that lets you sleep at night, knowing your site won’t tank the moment a spike hits. Don’t pick for the brand—pick for the fit.

Hopefully, you found something here that saves you time, money, or at least a few nerves. If so, mission accomplished. Thanks for reading—and good luck finding the stack that’s truly right for your story.

items.title: Fastest BSC RPC: GetBlock Achieves Top Speeds in the Americas
items.author: GetBlock
items.content:
GetBlock, a premium RPC node provider and Web3 infrastructure platform, has announced its first major achievement with region-specific shared APIs. Just two weeks after launch, New York-based BNB Smart Chain (BSC) infrastructure cluster has become the fastest in the Americas.

Fastest BSC RPC in the Americas: GetBlock reaches 21 ms latency in the US

As of July 15, 2025, GetBlock’s APIs have reached a significant milestone in the Western Hemisphere. According to a recent report from , the platform outperformed all competitors in both North and South America in terms of latency.\

\ In North America, GetBlock’s BSC mainnet node averaged a latency of 41.4 milliseconds for the method making it 8.2% faster than ConsenSys’ Infura, one of the oldest and most reputable RPC node providers.

In specific locations such as Northern Virginia, US, and Montreal, Canada, latency drops as low as an impressive 21 ms.

In South America, GetBlock is dominating the charts: recent benchmarking shows a latency of just 128.6 ms. Its closest competitor, blockchain heavyweight Alchemy, returns data over 35% slower.

\ Numbers speak for themselves: GetBlock now offers the fastest RPC endpoints for BSC in both North and South America - an impressive feat for one of the dominant EVM-compatible blockchains. For BSC-based dApps of all types, migrating to GetBlock is a smart move.

Earlier this month, GetBlock launched for its shared nodes. This allows users to specify the location of their RPC API connection when setting up an access token.

Getting started with GetBlock’s region-specific APIs: Just two steps

To start sending requests to the fastest BSC RPC nodes in the Americas, simply:

  1. Log in to GetBlock using MetaMask, GitHub, Google, or your email address.
  2. Navigate to the Shared Nodes menu. Click Get, then select the following:
  • Protocol: BNB Smart Chain
  • Interface: GraphQL, JSON-RPC, or WebSockets

That’s it! Your unique API endpoint (e.g., https://go.getblock.us/0ef4d242b…) will connect your dApp to the industry’s fastest RPC nodes.

This feature is available on all plans, including the free tier. Paid plans also benefit from along with top-tier speed.

And this is just the beginning.

Singapore endpoints and additional blockchains will soon be supported with region-specific API options.

items.title: Async Without the Headache: Meet areq, the Drop-In Replacement for Python’s Requests
items.author: Ganesh Palanikumar
items.content:
If you’ve written Python scripts that use , you know how intuitive and reliable it feels. It just works. It’s simple. And it’s become almost muscle memory at this point. The story starts with a simple piece of code that looks something like this.

import requests

def foo():
    try:
        response = requests.get("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1")
        assert isinstance(response, requests.Response)
        response.raise_for_status()
        if response.ok:
            response_json = response.json()
            return response_json
    except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
        response_text = response.text
        print("Text:", response_text)
        return None

if __name__ == '__main__':
    foo()

It’s clean, concise, and easily testable.

But then your application grows. Maybe you’re hitting hundreds of APIs, or building a web scraper. Anyway, your trusty synchronous code is no longer snappy. It starts to slow down. And someone suggests why not go async? The idea sticks in your head. It seems elegant, beautiful and most of all, inevitable. So, you start the refactor. You read up about , the de-facto library for anyone wanting to make async requests. And it does seem simple - at first. But after refactoring and debugging, you are left with this.

import httpx
import asyncio

async def foo():
    try:
        async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
            response = await client.get("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1")
            assert isinstance(response, httpx.Response)
            response.raise_for_status()
            if response.is_success:
                response_json = response.json()
                print(response_json)
    except httpx.HTTPError as e:
        print("Text:", getattr(response, 'text', None))
        return None

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(foo())

Great. You have joined the async revolution. But something feels weird. So many changes just to get the async flow to work!

  • context manager
  • returns a coroutine that must be awaited
  • exceptions raised are no longer requests.exceptions.RequestException
  • Minor issue, but is now replaced with

From a refactoring point of view, I am definitely more nervous and less confident about the code than the code. Not because is a bad library, but because the affected area is a lot. If something goes wrong, it is going to be very hard to debug. Plus, I no longer recognize the code or the idioms.

This was exactly the problem I was trying to solve. A way to move from synchronous workflows using requests to asynchronous workflows using in an elegant way.

This led me to build : a minimal async drop-in replacement for requests—no weird new interfaces, no extra ceremony. Just async where you want it, and familiar behavior where you expect it. Here’s the same code using areq instead of requests, with almost nothing changed.

import areq
import asyncio
import requests

async def foo():
    try:
        response = await areq.get("https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/todos/1")
        assert isinstance(response, requests.Response)
        response.raise_for_status()
        if response.ok:
            response_json = response.json()
            print(response_json)
    except requests.exceptions.RequestException as e:
        print("Text:", getattr(response, 'text', None))
        return None

if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(foo())

That’s it. No extra configs, no new types to learn. No special idioms. Yes, I had to add async-await wherever required. But almost nothing else has changed. What’s more, isinstance(response, requests.Response) returns True!

  • No boilerplate context managers
  • No new Response object to learn
  • Type checks and except clauses remain same

The goal behind is simple: Bring async benefits to your software without wrecking your code (or your brain). You get your requests ergonomics plus async speed—no rewrites, no API mental overhead. is nothing fancy: it is just a simple wrapper over . But I feel it will be really useful for some people.

🔄 Teams migrating to async gradually

You don’t always have the luxury to stop everything and refactor your entire codebase for async. With , you can start adopting async piece by piece, keeping the rest of your logic intact.

Want to convert just one function? One endpoint? One module? enables you to do just that, without breaking .ok, type assertions or your test mocks.

🧠 People who eventually want to move to (but not today)

isn’t here to compete with . In fact, it can be your on-ramp to .

You can use to unlock async performance now—without rewriting everything. Later, once you’ve stabilized your logic and gathered profiling data, you can move to native for deeper control or streaming needs. —

If you’re working on scrapers, bots, micro services or any software that needs concurrency but don’t want the full mental load of switching to a new request paradigm, is for you. It gives you async without ceremony.

💼 Teams maintaining legacy requests code

Your old requests code works fine. It’s battle-tested. Now you just want it to go faster. lets you do that. No rewrite. No breakage. No angry regression tests.

  • Uses under the hood.
  • Converts the response to a lookalike.
  • Converts common exceptions (e.g. ) into requests.exceptions.RequestException subclasses.
  • Supports all basic HTTP verbs

It’s not a full replacement for everything or can do, but it covers 90% of practical use cases, especially when you’re trying to modernize an old codebase.

  • Streaming and advanced session handling are not supported (yet!)
  • The response object is a subclass of , but cannot be treated as same in all scenarios. But hey—it’s early. And it works for most real-world use cases that I have encountered.

It is very easy to install . You can install it from PyPi just like any other python package.

Starring the repo helps more people discover , and lets you keep up to date with features as they roll out.

Take for a spin. Use it in your code, tell me what breaks. File an issue.

Want streaming support? Cookie handling? What is that one feature that will make your life so much better?

is open sourced under MIT license. It is also my project with me as the sole developer. As such, if you have a cool idea, do fork the repository, implement and put up a PR. I will definitely review it.

Tell your friends and colleagues. Blog about it - the good, the bad and the ugly. The more people who use , the better it is going to become.

Sometimes all you need is a small wrapper that respects your old habits. That’s . It won’t save the world, but it’ll save you a few afternoons.

  • wanted to “just make it async” without rewriting everything

  • been burned by subtle differences between and

  • Or you just want to make your trusty legacy code run a but faster

    Then might just be your new favorite micro-library.

items.title: Developers Are Missing Out on the Low-Code Shift
items.author: Maksym Kunytskyi
items.content:
"Code is power. But power without flexibility is weakness."

The world of development has changed. Yet many developers remain blind to it. While big studios automate internal workflows, freelancers build MVPs over weekends, and startups test ideas in three clicks, many devs still live in the illusion: "This isn't serious. This isn't real development."

Low-code Is Already Here — And It's Not Going Away

Webflow: The Designer Who Codes Better Than You

Webflow isn’t just a "site builder" — it’s a visual front-end framework. From animations to CMS to complex logic — all done without writing code. Add Wized or custom scripts, and it becomes a full-blown web app without touching React, Next.js, or even VS Code.

You could build a landing in a day — but you won’t, because "you don’t use site builders." Meanwhile, a no-code specialist sells an MVP for $5k — and the client returns with an investor.

Xano offers a complete backend: database, logic, authentication, cloud functions, REST API — no server setup, no CI/CD, no Jenkins. You’re not writing code — you’re building solutions.

And it’s not just — it’s .

ChatGPT + Codex = Code That Writes Itself

OpenAI launched Codex. ChatGPT became your pair programmer. Integrated with VS Code, it explains, debugs, suggests architecture, writes functions, translates code.

This isn’t "instead of you" — it’s . Actually, .

Still think you’re irreplaceable? Ask the typists, translators, accountants…

Most devs still laugh at no-code. Meanwhile, the startup world stopped laughing — and started scaling.

Why? Because denial is easier than adaptation. Because it's painful to admit that low-code does 80% of your job. Because mocking it on Reddit feels safer than learning something new.

But while you’re building a custom CRUD with Prisma + PostgreSQL + Docker + AWS — someone already raised funds with Webflow + Xano.

Coexistence Is Inevitable

You don’t have to become a no-code expert. But you :

✅ Understand how these tools work

✅ Use them for prototyping

✅ Integrate them into your stack

✅ Educate your product team

✅ Accept that this is the future

Otherwise — you’ll be part of the past.

"Low-code isn’t replacing development. It’s accelerating it. And in today’s world, speed is currency."

Someone, right now, is launching a startup with Webflow, Xano, AI integrations — and zero dev hours. And it’s happening more often.

The question isn’t low-code enters your workflow. The question is you’ll realize it.

🟢 Want to stay relevant in dev? Start by learning the tools you’ve been ignoring.

items.title: AI in the Classroom Promises Efficiency—But at What Cost to Learning?
items.author: Igboanugo David Ugochukwu
items.content:
The glow of her laptop screen cuts through the darkness of her dorm room. 2:47 AM. Sarah Chen's fingers hover over the refresh button, trembling slightly from too much caffeine and too little sleep. Click. The grade materializes—a stark number that feels like a verdict handed down by some invisible tribunal. Her essay on post-colonial literature, weeks of careful analysis exploring Achebe's linguistic rebellion and Adichie's narrative innovations, has been judged. Dissected. Scored.

By a machine that has never wept reading "Things Fall Apart."

This scene repeats itself across thousands of universities every night. Students receiving grades from systems that operate in the shadows, algorithmic judges that never sleep, never doubt, never feel the weight of human stories embedded in academic work. We've entered an era where artificial intelligence doesn't just assist education—it education.

The transformation is breathtaking in its scope. Grading systems that process submissions faster than any human could dream. Tutoring programs that adapt to individual learning styles with mathematical precision. Course designs optimized by algorithms that detect knowledge gaps before students even realize they exist. The promise gleams like fool's gold: personalized education at unprecedented scale, efficiency that could democratize learning for millions.

But scratch beneath this technological veneer, and you'll find questions that make educators lose sleep.

What happens when a student fundamentally disagrees with an AI-assigned grade? Can you argue with an algorithm? When machines misinterpret cultural context or fail to recognize creative brilliance that doesn't fit predetermined patterns, who bears responsibility? Does artificial intelligence represent the future of fair assessment—or the death of educational nuance?

These aren't theoretical concerns floating in academic journals. They're reshaping the DNA of higher education right now, transforming the sacred relationship between teacher and student into something unprecedented: a triangle involving human learners, human educators, and artificial minds that process but don't truly understand.

The Silent Revolution: How Machines Learned to Grade

Step into any computer science building at 3 AM, and you'll hear them—the quiet hum of servers processing endless streams of student code. Auto-grading systems have been the unsung heroes of programming education for decades, elegant machines that test, evaluate, and score with mechanical precision.

Dr. Briana Morrison knows this world intimately. As Associate Professor at the University of Virginia and Co-Chair of the ACM Education Board, she's witnessed the evolution firsthand. "Within computing education, we have used auto-graders or computer systems to test student submissions (i.e., the programs they submit for homework) for years," she explains with the matter-of-fact tone of someone describing a familiar tool. "Grades are always impartial."

These systems operate on beautiful simplicity:

import unittest

def student_function(x):
    return x * 2

class TestStudentFunction(unittest.TestCase):
    def test_double(self):
        self.assertEqual(student_function(2), 4)
        self.assertEqual(student_function(5), 10)

unittest.main()

Input. Process. Output. Binary perfection.

But here's where our story takes a sharp turn into uncharted territory. Educational institutions are no longer content with grading code. They want AI to evaluate essays that grapple with existential questions. Creative projects that defy standardization. Discussion posts that reveal the messy complexity of human thought.

Machine learning algorithms are being deployed to personalize learning at scales that would make traditional educators dizzy. ChatGPT answers student questions about quantum mechanics. GitHub Copilot helps aspiring programmers write their first algorithms. Gradescope processes thousands of exams simultaneously, identifying patterns that human graders might miss.

The efficiency is undeniable. The implications? Terrifying.

Tools that once supplemented human judgment are increasingly replacing it. And unlike the clear-cut world of code testing, subjective assessment involves nuance, context, and cultural understanding that even the most sophisticated AI systems struggle to grasp.

The question haunting education isn't whether AI will continue its march into our classrooms. It's whether we're prepared for what we might lose in the process.

The Gamble of Probabilistic Grading

There's a chasm between traditional auto-grading and modern AI assessment—a chasm that most educators haven't fully recognized. Traditional systems are deterministic. Feed them identical inputs, get identical outputs. Every single time. Generative AI systems? They're creatures of probability, statistical approximations wrapped in the illusion of certainty.

Morrison draws this distinction with the precision of a surgeon: "I believe the system you are referring to is one based purely on AI-powered grading… To my knowledge, no one in computing education is using one of these systems."

Her warning carries the weight of prophecy: "Even if one were trained only on course content, there is no guarantee that the probabilistic text and grade generated would match that of the expert or instructor."

Picture this scenario: A student submits a brilliant analysis of Shakespearean metaphor. The AI system—trained on thousands of similar essays—processes the submission through layers of neural networks, each layer adding its own statistical uncertainty. The final grade emerges not from logical reasoning but from pattern recognition, from the system's best statistical guess about what a human evaluator might decide.

Consider how this might work in practice:

{
  "prompt": "Grade this essay on World War II: [essay_text]",
